items.title: Anti-AChR
items.contentHtml:
Anti-AChR

  • Present in 80-90% of generalized and 50-60% ocular myasthenia.
  • They are IgG1 and IgG3, they activate complement and destroy the neuromuscular plaque.
  • The titer does not strictly correlate with clinical severity, but high titers suggest thymoma.
  • False positive: up to 5% in thymoma without myasthenia, lupus, rheumatoid arthritis.
  • Differential diagnosis: Lambert-Eaton syndrome (anti-voltage-dependent calcium channels).

items.title: Anti-MuSK
items.contentHtml:
Anti-MuSK

  • Positive in 40-70% of patients seronegative for anti-AChR.
  • Associated with a more severe phenotype with prominent bulbar involvement (dysarthria, dysphagia, facial weakness).
  • Poor response to anticholinesterases: may worsen with pyridostigmine.
  • Routine thymectomy is not indicated (not associated with thymoma).
  • Treatment of choice: corticosteroids and immunosuppressants (mycophenolate, rituximab).

items.foco: Ptosis and diplopia as initial presentation
items.title: ocular presentation
items.contentHtml:
Initial ptosis and diplopia

  • First symptom in 50-70% of cases; ptosis is usually asymmetrical and fluctuating.
  • Dropping eyelid sign (Cogan): after looking down and up, the eyelid rises briefly and then descends.
  • Binocular diplopia due to extraocular muscle fatigue, worsens with sustained gaze.
  • Differential diagnosis: third nerve palsy (fixed pupil), thyroid ophthalmopathy (eyelid retraction, proptosis).
  • 80% of pure ocular patients generalize within 2 years if they do not receive immunosuppression.

items.foco: Fluctuating muscle fatigue and worsens in the evening
items.title: Fluctuating fatigue
items.contentHtml:
Fatigability and evening worsening

  • Muscle weakness that increases with activity and improves with rest (true fatigue).
  • Typically worsens in the evening (daytime phenomenon) and improves in the morning.
  • Ice test: applying ice to the eyelid for 2 min improves ptosis in myasthenia (sensitivity 80%, specificity 97%).
  • Rest test: improvement in strength after 10-30 min of rest.
  • Differential diagnosis: chronic fatigue syndrome (not objective), metabolic myopathies (does not improve with rest).

items.title: Tensilon test
items.contentHtml:
Tensilon (edrophonium) test

  • Administer 2 mg intravenously as a bolus; if there is no reaction in 30-45 sec, give additional 8 mg.
  • Positive response: obvious improvement in weakness in 30-60 sec, lasts 5-10 minimum
  • Risk of cholinergic crisis and bradycardia: have atropine 0.5-1 mg intravenously ready.
  • False positives: amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Guillain-Barré syndrome, botulism.
  • False negatives: anti-MuSK positive, recent use of anticholinesterases.

items.title: electromyography with repetitive stimulation
items.contentHtml:
electromyography with repetitive stimulation

  • Stimulation at 2-3 Hz shows a >10% decrease in the amplitude of the compound muscle action potential.
  • The decrease is maximum between the 4th and 5th stimulus.
  • Single fiber electromyography (jitter) is more sensitive (>95%), but less specific.
  • Perform after suspending pyridostigmine 12-24 h to avoid false negatives.
  • Differential diagnosis: Lambert-Eaton syndrome shows >100% increase after tetanic stimulation or exercise.

items.title: Timoma
items.contentHtml:
Associated thymoma

  • Present in 10-15% of generalized myasthenia; up to 30% if anti-AChR positive.
  • Typical age: 40-60 years; more common in men.
  • Chest computed tomography with contrast is the study of choice (sensitivity >95%).
  • Masaoka Classification: stage I (encapsulated) to IV (metastasis).
  • All patients with myasthenia should have chest computed tomography at diagnosis to rule out thymoma.

items.foco: Myasthenic crisis due to infection or stress
items.title: myasthenic crisis
items.contentHtml:
Myasthenic crisis

  • Definition: acute exacerbation requiring ventilatory support (intubation or non-invasive ventilation).
  • Triggers: infection (30-50%), surgery, pregnancy, reduction of immunosuppressants, stress.
  • Management: intensive care unit admission, intravenous pyridostigmine (1/30 oral dose), plasmapheresis or intravenous immunoglobulin (2 g/kg).
  • Myasthenia Gravis Foundation of America classification scale to classify severity; class V = crisis.
  • differential diagnosis with cholinergic crisis: fasciculations, miosis, bradycardia, hypersalivation (response to atropine).

items.title: Pyridostigmine
items.contentHtml:
Pyridostigmine (Mestinon)

  • First-line symptomatic: initial dose 30-60 mg PO every 4-6 h; maximum 120 mg every 3-4 hours.
  • Reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, increases availability of ACh in the synaptic cleft.
  • Adverse effects: diarrhea, cramps, hypersalivation, bradycardia (management with anticholinergics such as atropine or glycopyrrolate).
  • Form extended release (180 mg) for nocturnal symptoms, not in crises.
  • Do not use in anti-MuSK (may worsen).

items.foco: Cholinergic crisis due to overdose of anticholinesterases
items.title: Cholinergic crisis
items.contentHtml:
Cholinergic crisis

  • Due to excess pyridostigmine or other anticholinesterases.
  • Muscarinic symptoms: miosis, bradycardia, hypersalivation, lacrimation, diarrhea, bronchorrhea.
  • Nicotinic symptoms: fasciculations, muscle weakness (includes muscles respiratory).
  • differential diagnosis with myasthenic crisis: trial with edrophonium (worsens cholinergic crisis) or response to atropine (improves muscarinic symptoms).
  • Management: discontinue anticholinesterases, ventilatory support, intravenous atropine.

items.foco: Avoid drugs that worsen myasthenia (aminoglycosides, beta blockers)
items.title: Contraindicated drugs
items.contentHtml:
Drugs that worsen myasthenia

  • Aminoglycosides: gentamicin, amikacin, tobramycin (neuromuscular blockade).
  • Beta blockers: propranolol, atenolol (may increase weakness).
  • Neuromuscular blockers: succinylcholine, vecuronium (risk). of prolonged paralysis).
  • Others: intravenous magnesium, penicillamine, quinine, procainamide, verapamil.
  • Caution with fluoroquinolones and macrolides (may exacerbate).

items.foco: Thymectomy indicated in thymoma or young patients
items.title: Thymectomy
items.contentHtml:
Thymectomy

  • Indicated in all patients with thymoma (regardless of age or severity).
  • In myasthenia without thymoma, indicated in patients 18-65 years old with positive anti-AChR and generalized myasthenia (MGTX study).
  • Benefit: long-term clinical improvement, reduces need for immunosuppressants.
  • Approach: median sternotomy or video-assisted thoracic surgery (thoracoscopy).
  • Not indicated in anti-MuSK, pure ocular myasthenia or >65 years with comorbidities.
